Representatives from signatory nations held their first meeting on September 19, 2022, at the IAC to discuss the Accords and cooperation in space more broadly.
With the accession of Thailand into the Accords on December 16, 2024, it became the first signatory to participate in both the International Lunar Research Station and the Artemis Accords, the second being Senegal.
In 2026, Portugal, Oman, Latvia, Jordan, Morocco, Malta, Ireland, Paraguay, Botswana, Serbia and Mauritius joined the Accords.
While doing so, signatories agree to coordinate with each other to provide appropriate protection for any proprietary and/or export-controlled information, and this provision does not extend to private sector operations unless conducted on behalf of a signatory.

Under the Artemis Accords, signatories intend to preserve outer space heritage, including historically significant sites and artifacts.

At the time of writing, 69 countries have signed the Artemis Accords including the U.S., the U.K., Japan, Italy, Canada, and Brazil."},{"url":"https://www.aip.org/fyi/us-artemis-accords-hit-50-signatories-in-2024","title":"US Artemis Accords Hit 50 Signatories in 2024 - AIP.ORG","content":"Austria and Panama signed the Artemis Accords today, bringing the total number of countries signed on to the United States’ flagship international space agreement to 50.
In addition to Austria and Panama, 15 countries joined the Artemis Accords in 2024: Belgium, Greece, Uruguay, Switzerland, Sweden, Slovenia, Lithuania, Peru, Slovakia, Armenia, Dominican Republic, Estonia, Cyprus, Chile, and Denmark.
Artemis I launched in 2022, and Artemis II is set to embark on a 10-day mission in April 2026.
Notably, China has not signed the Artemis Accords and plans to launch its own international lunar research station in the 2030s."}]}

As of June 25, 2026, with the accession of Botswana, 68 countries have signed the Accords, including 31 in Europe, 16 in Asia, 8 in South America, 5 in North America, 6 in Africa, and 2 in Oceania. Drafted by NASA and the U.S. Department of State, the Accords establish a framework for cooperation in the civil exploration and peaceful use of the Moon, Mars, and other astronomical objects. The Accords were originally signed on October 13, 2020, by representatives of the national space agencies of eight countries: Australia, Canada, Italy, Japan, Luxembourg, the United Arab Emirates, the United Kingdom, and the United States.","score":0.93161833,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://payloadspace.com/the-artemis-accords-by-the-numbers","title":"The Artemis Accords By the Numbers","content":"+ State of the Space Industry 2026. Oman announced this week that it had signed on to the Artemis Accords, becoming the 61st nation to agree to the set of non-binding principles. **A quick reminder:** The US led the establishment of the Artemis Accords in 2020. **Timeline:** 2024 was a banner year for the agreement, with 19 nations signing onto the accords—the most number of additions in a year. **What’s next:** There’s still room for the accords to grow its number of signatories in the years to come. Even though every nation doesn’t have a space program, US officials have said that non-spacefaring nations, many of whom rely on data collected in orbit, deserve a seat at the table when discussing the future of space exploration (though no one has said it’s a goal to have every nation sign on.). While European sovereignty may be driven by a response to the deterioration of geopolitical ties to the US, it’s opened the door for Middle Eastern space companies.","score":0.82082915,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.facebook.com/spacecom/posts/the-list-of-nations-signing-the-artemis-accords-is-growing-continuing-nasas-effo/1186472683343414","title":"Space.com - The list of nations signing the Artemis...","content":"## Space.com's Post. ### **Space.com**. It's easy to say you are agreeing to peaceful space exploration when you don't even have a space program.","score":0.43395805,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/eight-nations-sign-nasa-led-artemis-accords-guide-moon-exploration-n1243398","title":"Eight nations sign NASA-led Artemis Accords to guide moon exploration","content":"# Eight nations sign NASA-led Artemis Accords to guide moon exploration. NASA developed the Artemis Accords to partner with other nations to set basic principles to guide robotic and crewed lunar exploration. An illustration of Artemis astronauts on the moon.NASA. Eight nations have signed on to become founding members of NASA's Artemis Accords, an international agreement that establishes how countries can cooperate to peacefully and responsibly conduct exploration of the moon. “With today’s signing, we are uniting with our partners to explore the moon and are establishing vital principles that will create a safe, peaceful and prosperous future in space for all of humanity to enjoy,” Bridenstine said in a statement released Tuesday. The agreement’s name refers to NASA’s Artemis program, which aims to send astronauts, including the first woman, to the moon by 2024. “The Artemis journey is to the moon, but the destination of the Accords is a peaceful and prosperous future.”.","score":0.40042162,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.britannica.com/topic/Artemis-Accords","title":"Artemis Accords | NASA, United States, Outer Space Treaty, Criticism, Summary, Russia, & China | Britannica","content":"# Artemis Accords. The Artemis Accords are an international set of principles and nonbinding agreements first signed on October 13, 2020, to guide global cooperation in space exploration. ### Which countries were the initial signatories of the Artemis Accords? ### What are the stated objectives of the Artemis Accords? The stated objectives of the Artemis Accords include peaceful space exploration, transparency in national space policies, data sharing, assistance in space emergencies, registration of space objects, interoperability, establishing safety zones, limiting space debris, and preserving space heritage. **Artemis Accords**, set of principles and nonbinding agreements to guide international cooperation in space exploration. The title of the accords reflects their connection to the Artemis program, which aims to return astronauts to the Moon in the 2020s and eventually establish long-term exploration missions on the lunar surface. NASA anticipates international cooperation for future phases of the program, and for space exploration in general, and sought to prepare for it by establishing broad principles for the peaceful use of space through the accords.","score":0.37421933,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.79,"request_id":"578007a2-9b3b-4ab0-afc9-3f65745b8a8f"}
